question_text: Jammu and Kashmir's accession to India in October 1947 was signed by Maharaja Hari Singh under which specific condition that shaped subsequent constitutional arrangements?

options.text: Complete merger with India identical to all other princely states with no special status
Conditional accession on matters of defence, external affairs, and communications with the right to determine final status through a plebiscite
Accession only to Pakistan with India as a temporary administrator
Accession contingent on the British Crown continuing as constitutional head of J&K

explanation: Maharaja Hari Singh signed the Instrument of Accession on October 26, 1947 ceding control of defence, external affairs, and communications to India following the Pakistani tribal invasion supported by Pakistan. Governor General Mountbatten accepted the accession on the condition that the question of accession be settled by a reference to the people once order was restored. This conditional accession distinguished J&K from other princely states. The promised plebiscite never materialised, becoming a central grievance in Pakistan's position and a source of ongoing international dispute. The conditions of accession directly shaped J&K's subsequent special constitutional status.
elimination_hints: J&K accession was explicitly conditional; complete merger identical to other states was not the arrangement.
Hari Singh signed accession to India specifically, fleeing Pakistani-backed tribal invasion; accession to Pakistan is factually wrong.
British Crown was no longer involved in Indian constitutional matters by October 1947.
subject: Political Science

question_text: Article 370 of the Indian Constitution gave Jammu and Kashmir which special constitutional status that distinguished it from all other Indian states?

options.text: J&K was given the right to maintain a separate army and nuclear programme
J&K had its own Constitution and flag, and Parliament's laws applied to J&K only in matters of accession and with state government consent for other subjects
J&K was made an independent nation in permanent association with India
J&K was the only state where the Governor had absolute veto over all legislation

explanation: Article 370 gave J&K a unique constitutional status. The state had its own Constitution (adopted in 1956), its own flag, and a Sadr-e-Riyasat (President) rather than a Governor initially. Parliament could legislate for J&K only on matters specified in the Instrument of Accession (defence, foreign affairs, communications) directly, and on other subjects only with the concurrence of the J&K government. This meant a large body of central legislation including many Constitutional amendments did not automatically apply to J&K. The Article was framed as temporary but became a permanent fixture until its abrogation in August 2019.
elimination_hints: J&K did not have a separate army or nuclear programme; defence remained a central subject.
J&K was an integral part of India, not an independent nation; Article 370 defined the terms of integration.
The Governor's veto was not the defining feature of Article 370; legislative consent was the key mechanism.
subject: Political Science

question_text: Sheikh Abdullah, the Lion of Kashmir, was dismissed as Prime Minister of J&K in 1953 and imprisoned. What had been his primary political position that led to his dismissal?

options.text: He had been openly supporting Pakistan's position on the Kashmir dispute
He had been exploring the option of independence for Kashmir rather than integration with India
He had been demanding immediate merger of J&K with India on the same terms as other states
He had been calling for a military alliance between J&K and China

explanation: Sheikh Abdullah was dismissed and arrested in August 1953 because the Nehru government became concerned that he was exploring the possibility of an independent Kashmir. His speeches had begun to raise questions about whether complete integration with India was in Kashmir's best interest and his contacts with American officials raised suspicions of international backing for independence. The government used the J&K Constitution's provisions to have him dismissed by the Sadr-e-Riyasat. Abdullah spent many years in detention and exile. He eventually returned to become Chief Minister again in 1975 after signing the Kashmir Accord with Indira Gandhi.
elimination_hints: Sheikh Abdullah was the most prominent Kashmiri leader who had supported accession to India in 1947; supporting Pakistan was the opposite of his stance.
Demanding identical merger with India was not his position; he sought to maintain and expand Kashmir's autonomy.
Alliance with China was never Sheikh Abdullah's political position.
subject: Political Science

question_text: The insurgency in Jammu and Kashmir that began in 1989 was significantly fuelled by which external development that provided resources, training, and motivation to militants?

options.text: The Soviet Union's collapse providing surplus weapons from Afghanistan
The withdrawal of Soviet forces from Afghanistan freeing Pakistan-trained mujahideen fighters and weapons for use in Kashmir
The United Nations Security Council passing a resolution authorising armed resistance in Kashmir
China directly supplying weapons to Kashmiri separatists through Aksai Chin

explanation: The insurgency that began in 1989 was significantly fuelled by the aftermath of the Soviet withdrawal from Afghanistan in 1989. Pakistan's Inter-Services Intelligence had spent the 1980s supporting Afghan mujahideen fighters against Soviet forces with American and Saudi funding. When Soviet forces withdrew, battle-hardened fighters, weapons, and the jihadi infrastructure were redirected toward Kashmir. Pakistan's support for militant groups in Kashmir through training, weapons, and logistics intensified massively in 1989-90. Combined with domestic Kashmiri grievances about rigged elections and political alienation, this external support transformed local discontent into a sustained armed insurgency.
elimination_hints: Soviet weapons did become available but the direct link was through Pakistan's redirection of Afghan mujahideen infrastructure.
No UN Security Council resolution authorised armed resistance in Kashmir; the UN resolutions called for plebiscite not armed conflict.
China has not been a direct arms supplier to Kashmiri insurgents; Pakistan has been the primary external supporter.
subject: Political Science

question_text: The erosion of J&K's special status through gradual extension of central laws before the 2019 abrogation is described by scholars as 'incremental integration'. What was the political consequence of this approach?

options.text: Incremental integration successfully reconciled Kashmiris to full integration with India without generating political opposition
Each incremental extension without Kashmiri consent deepened alienation and reinforced perceptions that J&K's autonomy was being unilaterally eroded, undermining democratic legitimacy
Incremental integration was judicially approved by the Supreme Court eliminating all constitutional objections
Incremental integration accelerated Kashmiri economic development making the population more supportive of integration

explanation: The incremental extension of central laws and institutions to J&K, often through executive orders under Article 370's clause 1(d) requiring Presidential Orders, was politically counterproductive despite its legal mechanisms. Each extension that bypassed genuine Kashmiri consent reinforced the perception among Kashmiri political leaders and public that Article 370's promise of conditional integration was being hollowed out unilaterally. The dismissal of Sheikh Abdullah in 1953 was followed by decades of central manipulation of J&K elections, imposition of compliant governments, and gradual erosion of autonomy. This repeated breach of the autonomy compact generated the very alienation that made the population more susceptible to separatist appeals when the 1989 insurgency began.
elimination_hints: Historical evidence shows incremental integration generated rather than resolved Kashmiri political opposition.
The Supreme Court did not give blanket approval to incremental integration; legal challenges were ongoing.
Economic development in J&K remained limited and uneven; economic benefits did not offset political alienation.
subject: Political Science

question_text: The 1987 J&K state elections are considered a significant trigger for the 1989 insurgency. What happened in the 1987 elections that contributed to this outcome?

options.text: The elections were boycotted by all parties making their results illegitimate
Widespread rigging of the elections allowed the Congress-NC alliance to win despite strong performance by the Muslim United Front, alienating a generation of politically active Kashmiris
The elections brought a strongly pro-Pakistan government to power in Srinagar
The central government refused to hold elections in 1987 imposing President's Rule instead

explanation: The 1987 J&K elections are widely considered a critical trigger for the subsequent insurgency. The Muslim United Front, a coalition of various parties, performed strongly in polling but the results were extensively manipulated in favour of the ruling Farooq Abdullah-Congress alliance. Polling officers were replaced, ballot boxes were stuffed, and MUF candidates who had won were declared losers. Many young MUF workers and candidates who had tried to work within the democratic system concluded that democratic politics in J&K was irredeemably rigged against them. Several became founding members of militant organisations like the Jammu Kashmir Liberation Front that launched the 1989 insurgency, making the 1987 rigging a direct radicalisation trigger.
elimination_hints: The 1987 elections were held with participation from multiple parties; a boycott did not occur.
The resulting government was the Farooq Abdullah-Congress alliance, not a pro-Pakistan government.
Elections were held in 1987; the central government did not impose President's Rule instead.
subject: Political Science

question_text: The Kashmir conflict involves overlapping and sometimes contradictory claims from multiple parties. Which description best maps the principal parties and their positions?

options.text: India claims full sovereignty; Pakistan claims J&K based on Muslim majority; Kashmiri separatists seek independence; Kashmiri Pandits want return to valley
India claims full sovereignty; Pakistan claims J&K; China claims only Aksai Chin; all Kashmiris uniformly support independence
India claims J&K but acknowledges Pakistan's right to Pakistani Kashmir; all other parties accept this framework
The conflict is entirely between India and Pakistan with Kashmiris having no distinct political positions

explanation: The Kashmir conflict is genuinely multi-party with distinct and partially overlapping claims. India claims full sovereignty over J&K based on the Instrument of Accession and democratic integration. Pakistan claims J&K based on the two-nation theory arguing a Muslim-majority state should be with Pakistan, and administers Pakistan Occupied Kashmir. Kashmiri separatists within the valley range from pro-Pakistan groups to pro-independence groups (azadi), with the Hurriyat Conference representing the latter. Kashmiri Pandits, who fled the valley in large numbers in 1990 following militant threats, represent a displaced community seeking return and justice. China additionally claims Aksai Chin and has administered it since 1962. This multi-party structure makes resolution extraordinarily complex.
elimination_hints: Not all Kashmiris support independence; the political landscape is diverse including pro-India, pro-Pakistan, and pro-independence factions.
China's claims extend beyond Aksai Chin to include parts of Ladakh; and Kashmiri positions are not uniform.
Kashmiris have distinctly articulated political positions that are not reducible to the India-Pakistan bilateral framework.
subject: Political Science

question_text: The abrogation of Article 370 and bifurcation of J&K into two Union Territories in August 2019 was a major constitutional change. Critically evaluate the constitutional procedure used.

options.text: The procedure was fully constitutional because Parliament has unlimited power to amend the Constitution

The procedure was legally contested because it used Presidential Orders to declare the J&K Constituent Assembly's concurrence was no longer needed, effectively using Article 370 to abrogate itself, bypassing the requirement for J&K's consent
The procedure was straightforward because Article 370 had always been a temporary provision that could be removed by simple parliamentary majority
The procedure was illegal because the Supreme Court declared it unconstitutional within months of the abrogation

explanation: The constitutional procedure used for abrogating Article 370 was legally innovative and contested. The government used a Presidential Order to declare that the J&K Constituent Assembly's functions would be deemed to be exercised by the J&K legislature, which was then under President's Rule meaning the Governor exercised its functions, who acted on central government advice. This allowed the Centre to give its own concurrence through the Governor, effectively using Article 370 itself to remove Article 370 without genuine J&K consent. Critics argued this circular procedure violated the spirit and intent of the provision which required genuine J&K concurrence. The Supreme Court upheld the abrogation in December 2023 but the constitutional questions remain debated.
elimination_hints: Parliament's constitutional amendment power is not unlimited; Article 370 required specific procedures including J&K concurrence.
Article 370's temporary character was disputed; its abrogation required a specific procedure that was legally contested.
The Supreme Court upheld rather than struck down the abrogation, though in December 2023 not within months.
subject: Political Science

question_text: Comparing the Kashmir conflict with the successful resolution of the Mizo insurgency, what fundamental difference explains why a similar negotiated settlement has proved impossible in Kashmir?

options.text: Kashmiri leaders have been personally less willing to compromise than Laldenga was
The Kashmir conflict is internationalised with Pakistan as an active external party and a UN Security Council dimension making it structurally different from the purely internal Mizo conflict
Kashmir's Muslim majority makes religious compromise impossible in a way that the Christian Mizo community's demands were not
The Indian government has been more willing to negotiate with the Mizo than with Kashmiris due to the smaller strategic importance of Mizoram

explanation: The most fundamental structural difference is the internationalisation of the Kashmir conflict. Mizoram was purely an internal Indian conflict between the Indian state and Mizo insurgents; Pakistan had no stake, role, or position in it. The resolution was therefore achievable through bilateral negotiation between the Indian government and Laldenga. Kashmir involves Pakistan as an active external party that administers Pakistani Kashmir, supports insurgent groups, raises the issue internationally, and has fought three wars with India over J&K. The UN Security Council has resolutions calling for plebiscite. No internal settlement between India and Kashmiri leaders can be durable without addressing Pakistan's position and the international dimension. This external internationalisation makes Kashmir structurally impossible to resolve through the bilateral internal negotiation that worked in Mizoram.
elimination_hints: Leader personality is secondary; structural internationalisation is the key difference.
Religious identity alone does not prevent settlement; the external party dimension is the decisive structural factor.
Strategic importance differences are real but the internationalisation dimension is the more fundamental barrier.
subject: Political Science

question_text: The migration of Kashmiri Pandits from the Kashmir valley in 1989-1990 is a contested historical event with different narratives. Which assessment most accurately captures what the historical evidence supports?

options.text: The migration was entirely voluntary with no threats or violence against Pandits from any source

The migration resulted from a combination of targeted killings by militants, threatening broadcasts, and an atmosphere of fear, with debate about the role of Governor Jagmohan's administration in facilitating or accelerating the exodus
The migration was entirely orchestrated by the Indian government to empty the valley of Hindus deliberately
The migration was solely caused by the J&K government encouraging Pandits to leave to delegitimise the insurgency

explanation: The historical evidence on the Kashmiri Pandit exodus is genuinely complex. Targeted killings of prominent Pandits by militants did occur and created genuine fear. Threatening broadcasts from mosques occurred in some areas. An atmosphere of fear was real and documented. At the same time, the role of Governor Jagmohan, who had just taken charge and whose administration's decisions during January-February 1990 are debated, remains contested: some accounts suggest he facilitated the departure by assuring Pandits of safety in camps while providing no such assurances in the valley, others dispute this. The exodus was real, its consequences for the Pandit community devastating, and demands for return and justice are legitimate. Reducing it to either a purely spontaneous flight or a pure government conspiracy misrepresents the available evidence.
elimination_hints: Targeted killings and threats against Pandits are well documented; voluntary migration without any threat is factually wrong.
While government policies played a role, the deliberate orchestration narrative is contested and not established by evidence.
The J&K government's role is one factor in a multi-causal event; reducing it to a single government conspiracy oversimplifies.
subject: Political Science

question_text: Match the key events and persons in Column A with their correct descriptions related to Kashmir in Column B.
explanation: Maharaja Hari Singh signed the Instrument of Accession to India in October 1947 following the Pakistani-backed tribal invasion. Sheikh Abdullah, the Lion of Kashmir, led the National Conference and was first Prime Minister of J&K before dismissal and imprisonment in 1953, returning as Chief Minister in 1975. Article 370 provided J&K its special constitutional status including its own Constitution and flag until the 2019 abrogation. The 1987 elections are widely identified as a trigger for the 1989 insurgency due to their rigging alienating a generation of political activists.
elimination_hints: Hari Singh was the ruler who signed accession; Sheikh Abdullah was the political leader of the Kashmiri people.
Article 370 was specifically the constitutional provision, not a political event or personality.
1987 elections are specifically linked to insurgency trigger through the rigging narrative.
subject: Political Science

column_b.text: Widely rigged poll that alienated Kashmiri youth and triggered the 1989 insurgency
Constitutional provision giving J&K special status until its abrogation in 2019
Ruler of J&K who signed Instrument of Accession to India in October 1947
National Conference leader called Lion of Kashmir; jailed 1953; returned as CM 1975

question_text: Match the phases of the Kashmir issue in Column A with their dominant political dynamic in Column B.
explanation: Phase 1 saw J&K's special status established with Sheikh Abdullah as PM, giving the accession democratic legitimacy through the National Conference government. Phase 2 after Sheikh's 1953 dismissal was characterised by the Centre installing compliant governments and incrementally eroding Article 370 protections, deepening political alienation. Phase 3 saw Sheikh's return under the 1975 Kashmir Accord but culminated in the 1987 election rigging that triggered a new cycle of alienation. Phase 4 from 1989 onwards is characterised by Pakistan-backed armed insurgency, AFSPA application, security operations, and the long process of conflict management without resolution.
elimination_hints: Phase 1 was the legitimising period with Sheikh as democratic head, not the alienation period.
Phase 2's defining feature was the erosion of autonomy through compliant governments, not insurgency.
The 1989 insurgency phase is specifically about armed conflict with external support, not political accommodation.
subject: Political Science

column_b.text: Pakistan-backed militants and local alienation combine; AFSPA and security operations dominate
Compliant governments installed; incremental erosion of Article 370 autonomy; political alienation deepens
Special status established; Sheikh as PM; accession legitimised through democratic governance
Sheikh returns as CM under Kashmir Accord; 1987 election rigging triggers new alienation cycle

question_text: Match the parties to the Kashmir conflict in Column A with their stated position on its resolution in Column B.
explanation: India's position is that J&K is an integral part of India, the matter is settled by the Instrument of Accession, and no external mediation is needed given the Simla Agreement's bilateral framework. Pakistan's position is that the UN resolutions calling for plebiscite remain valid, that Pakistani Kashmir was legitimately taken as part of Pakistan's position on partition, and that the issue remains internationally unresolved. The Hurriyat Conference represents the separatist spectrum demanding either azadi (independence) or merger with Pakistan. Kashmiri Pandits, as displaced persons, demand the right to dignified return to their homes in the valley and justice for the killings and displacement they suffered in 1989-90.
elimination_hints: India's position specifically rejects external mediation and uses the Simla Agreement framework.
Pakistan's position specifically invokes UN resolutions; it is not simply demanding annexation.
Hurriyat represents the separatist spectrum; Kashmiri Pandits have a distinct and different set of demands.
subject: Political Science

column_b.text: Demand dignified return to Kashmir valley and justice for those killed during migration
J&K is an integral part of India; no external mediation; bilateral Simla Agreement framework
UN plebiscite should be held; Pakistani Kashmir is legitimately part of Pakistan's position
Azadi (independence) or merger with Pakistan; rejection of Indian constitutional integration

question_text: Assertion (A): Sheikh Abdullah's initial support for J&K's accession to India rather than Pakistan was not based on religious affiliation but on his secular democratic vision and trust in Nehru's commitment to Kashmiri autonomy. Reasoning (R): Sheikh Abdullah led a secular National Conference that had fought against the Maharaja's feudal rule; he preferred democratic secular India over theocratic Pakistan and believed Nehru would honour J&K's special status.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

explanation: Both statements are historically accurate and causally connected. Sheikh Abdullah's decision to support accession to India rather than Pakistan was indeed based on secular democratic values rather than religious considerations. As a Muslim leader choosing India, he represented the position that Kashmiri identity and interests were better served by secular democratic India than by Pakistan's Islamic state identity. His National Conference had a secular, pro-poor, anti-feudal ideology. His close personal friendship with Nehru and trust in Nehru's promises of autonomy were critical to his decision. The Reasoning correctly identifies both ideological alignment with secular India and personal trust in Nehru as the factors explaining the Assertion.
subject: Political Science

question_text: Assertion (A): The Kashmir conflict demonstrates that democratic deficits including election rigging can be more radicalising than economic grievances alone. Reasoning (R): The 1987 election rigging directly produced militants who had previously participated in electoral politics but concluded that the democratic system was irredeemably stacked against them.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

explanation: Both statements are analytically sound and causally connected. The Kashmir insurgency's origins in the 1987 election rigging provides a powerful illustration that denial of democratic participation can be a more immediate radicalisation trigger than economic deprivation. Economic grievances in Kashmir were real but had existed for decades without producing mass insurgency. The 1987 elections offered politically engaged Kashmiris a democratic channel and its deliberate manipulation converted believers in constitutional politics into militants. JKLF founder Yasin Malik had been an MUF election worker; several other militants had similar democratic political backgrounds before 1987. The Reasoning precisely identifies this specific mechanism, making the causal connection between the Assertion and the 1987 evidence direct.
subject: Political Science

question_text: Assertion (A): The Simla Agreement of 1972 between India and Pakistan fundamentally changed the framework for resolving the Kashmir dispute. Reasoning (R): By establishing that J&K should be resolved bilaterally between India and Pakistan without third-party mediation, the Simla Agreement effectively set aside the UN resolutions calling for plebiscite as the operative framework.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

explanation: Both statements are accurate and causally connected. The Simla Agreement signed by Indira Gandhi and Zulfikar Ali Bhutto in July 1972 following India's victory in the 1971 Bangladesh war was indeed a fundamental change in framework. By committing both countries to resolve Kashmir bilaterally and not involving third parties, it effectively displaced the UN plebiscite framework that Pakistan had previously invoked. India has consistently cited Simla as establishing that Kashmir is a bilateral matter and that UN resolutions are superseded. Pakistan has ambiguously maintained both the Simla bilateral framework and the UN plebiscite demand, creating an ongoing interpretive dispute about which framework governs. The Reasoning correctly explains how Simla changed the operative framework.
subject: Political Science

question_text:
Arrange the following events in the history of Kashmir in correct chronological order:
1. Sheikh Abdullah returns as Chief Minister after Kashmir Accord with Indira Gandhi
2. Instrument of Accession signed by Maharaja Hari Singh
3. Sheikh Abdullah dismissed and imprisoned
4. Armed insurgency begins in Kashmir valley
5. Article 370 incorporated into Indian Constitution giving J&K special status
explanation: The correct chronological sequence is: Instrument of Accession signed October 1947; Article 370 incorporated into the Constitution in 1949; Sheikh Abdullah dismissed and imprisoned August 1953; Sheikh Abdullah returns as Chief Minister in 1975 after the Kashmir Accord with Indira Gandhi; armed insurgency begins in 1989. This sequence shows the political arc from accession through constitutional integration, through the long period of Sheikh's imprisonment and alienation, through the attempt at reconciliation in 1975, and ultimately to the insurgency triggered by continued alienation and the 1987 election rigging.
elimination_hints: The Instrument of Accession in 1947 was the foundational event preceding all others.
Article 370 was incorporated in 1949 before Sheikh's dismissal in 1953.
Sheikh's return in 1975 came after his long imprisonment period, not before.
subject: Political Science

question_text:
Arrange the following causes of the 1989 Kashmir insurgency from the most deep-rooted historical cause to the most immediate proximate trigger:
1. Rigged 1987 J&K elections converting democratic activists into militants
2. Conditional nature of 1947 accession and unfulfilled plebiscite promise
3. Incremental erosion of Article 370 autonomy without Kashmiri consent from 1953
4. Afghan mujahideen infrastructure becoming available after Soviet withdrawal in 1989
5. Dismissal and imprisonment of Sheikh Abdullah in 1953 breaking the autonomy compact
explanation: The causal depth sequence runs from fundamental to proximate. The most foundational cause is the conditional 1947 accession with unfulfilled plebiscite promise creating a legitimacy deficit from the start. Sheikh Abdullah's 1953 dismissal was the first concrete breach of the autonomy compact. The incremental erosion of Article 370 autonomy from 1953 onward deepened alienation across three decades. The 1987 election rigging was the immediate radicalising trigger converting democratic activists into militants. The availability of Afghan mujahideen infrastructure in 1989 was the enabling factor that allowed domestic alienation to become organised armed insurgency rather than remaining political protest. This sequence shows how historical grievances accumulated before the external enabling factor arrived.
elimination_hints: The conditional 1947 accession is the deepest historical root predating all subsequent political failures.
The 1987 election rigging was the immediate radicalisation trigger, second-to-last in depth.
The Afghan mujahideen infrastructure was the external enabling factor, the most proximate cause.
subject: Political Science

question_text: Identify which of the following statements about Kashmir is TRUE: (I) The Instrument of Accession signed in 1947 gave India full and unconditional sovereignty over all of Jammu and Kashmir including Pakistan Occupied Kashmir. (II) Sheikh Abdullah was the founding leader of the Jammu and Kashmir National Conference. (III) The Simla Agreement of 1972 was signed between Indira Gandhi and Zulfikar Ali Bhutto following India's victory in the 1971 Bangladesh war. (IV) Article 370 was completely removed from the Indian Constitution by a constitutional amendment requiring two-thirds majority of both Houses.

options.text: Statements II and III
Statement III only
Statements I and III
Statement II only

explanation: Statements II and III are both true. Sheikh Abdullah did found the National Conference, transforming it from the Muslim Conference. The Simla Agreement was signed by Indira Gandhi and Zulfikar Ali Bhutto in July 1972 after India's victory in the Bangladesh war. Statement I is false because the Instrument of Accession was conditional on defence, external affairs, and communications, and did not resolve the question of Pakistan Occupied Kashmir which Pakistan administered from 1947. Statement IV is false because Article 370 was not removed through a constitutional amendment; it was abrogated through a Presidential Order under Article 370 itself combined with a Parliamentary resolution, which is why the procedure was legally contested.
subject: Political Science

question_text: Study the diagram showing the Kashmir conflict's multiple layers including the bilateral India-Pakistan dimension, the internal J&K political dimension, and the human rights dimension. Based on this diagram, why has a comprehensive resolution proved impossible despite decades of effort?

options.text: Resolution has been impossible because neither India nor Pakistan genuinely wants to resolve the conflict

Each layer of the conflict has different parties and different requirements for resolution that must all be satisfied simultaneously, creating a multi-dimensional puzzle where solutions to one layer can worsen another
Resolution is impossible because the conflict is fundamentally religious and religious conflicts cannot be resolved through political negotiation
Resolution has been blocked solely by United States and Chinese interference preventing bilateral settlement

explanation: The multi-layered diagram reveals why comprehensive resolution is so difficult: each layer has distinct requirements that can conflict with solutions to other layers. Resolving the India-Pakistan bilateral dimension requires territorial compromise that neither side's domestic politics allows. Addressing Kashmiri political aspirations for greater autonomy or independence conflicts with India's sovereignty position. Resolving the Kashmiri Pandit displacement requires security guarantees in the valley that insurgency conditions make impossible. Addressing human rights concerns requires security normalisation that Pakistan's continued support for militants prevents. Any movement on one layer creates complications on others: greater autonomy for Kashmir emboldens separatists; security operations needed against Pakistan-backed militants generate human rights concerns that fuel more alienation. This multi-dimensional interlocking nature is the structural reason for resolution's elusiveness.
elimination_hints: Both India and Pakistan have engaged in multiple peace processes; the failure is structural not motivational.
Religious dimensions exist but religious conflicts have been resolved elsewhere; the multi-layer structure is the specific barrier here.
External powers have played roles but the primary barriers are the interlocking internal layers of the conflict.
subject: Political Science
